questions.q: Is the Forward the market's price prediction of the Spot in the future?
questions.a:
**No. The Forward is the cost of replication.**

If you want to own the stock in 6 months, you have two choices:
1. Buy it in 6 months (you don't know the price).
2. Buy it **today** at the Spot price.

But buying today means you need to **borrow cash** (paying interest) and **carry the stock** (receiving dividends). The dividend collected reduces the cost of carry.

**F = S × e^{(r − q) × T}**

The Forward is not a forecast — it's an arbitrage-enforced price that reflects the cost of financing minus the benefit of holding.

questions.q: Stock XYZ's 6-month Forward trades at a 5% discount to Spot. Why?
questions.a:
**Two main reasons: high repo rate or high dividend yield.**

Usually F > S because of financing costs. But when the **benefit of holding** the stock (dividends + repo income from lending shares) **outweighs** the cost of borrowing cash, the Forward is pulled below the Spot.

F = S × e^{(r − q − repo) × T}

If (q + repo) > r, then the exponent is negative → **F < S**.

questions.q: A company slashes its dividend from €4 to €2. Spot stays flat. What happens to your Call options?
questions.a:
**Call prices increase.**

A call is **long the forward**. When dividends decrease, the forward price **increases** (less dividend drag on the carry). Higher forward → higher call value.

Even though the Spot didn't move, the forward shifted up, and all call strikes benefit. Puts decrease for the same reason.

questions.q: Spot = 100, r = 5%, no dividends. The 1Y Forward trades at 110. How do you arbitrage?
questions.a:
**Cash-and-Carry Arbitrage.** Fair Forward = 100 × (1 + 5%) = **105**. At 110, the Forward is overpriced by 5.

**Execution:**
1. **Borrow** €100 at 5% for 1 year.
2. **Buy** the stock at 100.
3. **Sell** the 1Y Forward at 110.

**At maturity:**
- Deliver the stock via the Forward → receive 110.
- Repay the loan → pay 105.
- **Risk-free profit = 5.**

questions.q: You buy a 1Y Forward. A €5 dividend is paid at 6 months as expected. Spot drops by €5. Does your Forward price change?
questions.a:
**No.** The Forward price remains unchanged (assuming no other market moves).

The dividend was already **priced into** the Forward at inception. When the stock goes ex-dividend:
- Spot drops by €5 (expected).
- But q (the dividend yield) is also gone — it no longer drags the Forward.

These two effects **exactly offset**. There is no P&L impact at the moment of payment.

questions.q: Same setup, but the company surprises with a €6 dividend instead of the expected €5. What happens?
questions.a:
**The Forward drops by approximately €1** (the unexpected portion).

- At T₀, the Forward was built pricing in €5 of dividends.
- The Spot drops by €6 (the actual dividend).
- But the Forward only had "protection" for €5.
- The extra €1 drop is **new information** that wasn't hedged.

The Forward must adjust down by the **surprise component** (€6 − €5 = €1). This is why unexpected dividend changes create P&L on forward/option positions.

questions.q: Vol = 20%, Spot = 100. Which is cheaper: a 3M ATM straddle or a 1Y ATM call?
questions.a:
**They cost the same ≈ 8.**

Using the ATM approximation:
- **Straddle (3M)** = 0.8 × S × σ × √T = 0.8 × 100 × 0.20 × √0.25 = 0.8 × 100 × 0.20 × 0.5 = **8**
- **ATM Call (1Y)** = 0.4 × S × σ × √T = 0.4 × 100 × 0.20 × 1.0 = **8**

Note: a straddle = call + put, and the ATM approximation gives Straddle ≈ 0.8 × S × σ × √T, while a single ATM call ≈ 0.4 × S × σ × √T. The straddle's shorter maturity compensates for having two legs.

questions.q: Which is more expensive: 1 Call strike 100 or 2 Calls strike 200?
questions.a:
**1 Call strike 100 is more expensive**, because of **gamma convexity**.

Talking about convexity abstractly doesn't help — here's a concrete example. Assume 3 equally likely outcomes: Spot = 100, 200, or 300.

| Scenario | 1 × Call(K=100) | 1 × Call(K=200) |
|---|---|---|
| S_T = 100 | 0 | 0 |
| S_T = 200 | 100 | 0 |
| S_T = 300 | 200 | 100 |
| **Average** | **300 / 3 = 100** | **100 / 3 = 33** |

So 2 × Call(K=200) = 2 × 33 = **66**.

**1 Call(K=100) = 100 > 66 = 2 Calls(K=200).**

The lower-strike call benefits more from large moves because its payoff is **linear in S** over a wider range. This is the convexity advantage.

questions.q: You are gamma long, delta hedged. Which path makes more money: +3%, +3%, +3% or 0%, 0%, +9%?
questions.a:
**Path 2 makes more money.** When gamma long, P&L scales with the **square** of the move.

**Square each daily move and sum:**
- Path 1: 3² + 3² + 3² = 9 + 9 + 9 = **27**
- Path 2: 0² + 0² + 9² = 0 + 0 + 81 = **81**

Path 2 is **3× more profitable** despite having the same total move (9%).

This is the key insight: gamma P&L is **convex** in the move size. One large move is worth more than many small moves that add up to the same total. This is why gamma traders love jumps and hate grinding markets.

questions.q: Worst-of on Apple ($200) and Nokia ($5): Apple drops $20, Nokia drops $1. Which is the Worst-off?
questions.a:
**Nokia is the Worst-off.**

The Worst-of is determined by **relative performance (percentage)**, not absolute dollar moves:
- Apple: $20 / $200 = **10% drop**
- Nokia: $1 / $5 = **20% drop**

Nokia dropped more in percentage terms → Nokia is the Worst-off component.

This is a common trap in interviews — the absolute dollar move is irrelevant. The Worst-of always compares percentage returns from the initial fixing level.

discount_cert.title: Trading Q&A — Discount Certificates
discount_cert.subtitle: Replication: DC = Long Stock − Call(K=Cap). **Client** buys the cert (Long S, Short Call). **Trader** sells the cert and hedges (Short S, Long Call).

questions.q: As a client, are you long or short volatility?
questions.a:
**Short volatility.** The client is short a call (strike = cap). When vol rises, that call becomes more expensive → the client's short position loses → the certificate drops in value. The client wants vol to stay low or decrease.

**The trader (issuer) is the opposite: long volatility** via the long call in their hedge. If vol rises, the trader's long call gains value — but this is offset by the certificate liability. The trader is delta-hedging, so the vol exposure depends on how well they manage the hedge.

questions.q: How is a Discount Certificate different from a covered call?
questions.a:
**Economically identical for the client.** A covered call = Long Stock + Short Call(K). Same payoff as the Discount Certificate. The difference is packaging: the certificate is a single security, while the covered call is two legs.

**For the trader**, selling a Discount Certificate is like buying back a covered call from the client. The trader ends up Long Call + Short Stock (after hedging), which they then delta-hedge dynamically.

questions.q: As a trader, where are you gamma long / gamma short?
questions.a:
**The trader is gamma LONG** (from the long call in their hedge).

The client is gamma short (short call). But the trader, having sold the cert and hedged with a long call, has **positive gamma** — meaning spot moves in either direction generate mark-to-market gains (before theta cost). Gamma is largest near the cap strike and near expiry.

The trader **pays theta** for this gamma — the long call decays. This is the classic long-gamma / short-theta profile.

questions.q: When does holding the stock outperform the Discount Certificate?
questions.a:
**When the stock rallies well above the cap.** The certificate payoff is capped at Cap, so any upside beyond that is lost. Below the cap, the certificate always outperforms because the client bought at a discount.

For the **trader**: a rally above the cap is favorable — the long call in the hedge pays off, and the certificate liability is capped. The trader's risk is a drop in the stock (short stock in hedge).

questions.q: What is the client's delta? What is the trader's delta?
questions.a:
**Client delta: between 0 and 1.** Long stock (+1) minus short call (−Δ_call). If the stock is well below the cap, the call is OTM → client delta ≈ 1. Near/above the cap → delta drops toward 0.

**Trader delta: between −1 and 0** (before hedging). Short stock (−1) plus long call (+Δ_call). The trader delta-hedges by buying shares. Near the cap at expiry, both the client and trader face a **delta discontinuity** (jumps from ~1 to 0 or vice versa), making hedging expensive — this is **pin risk**.

questions.q: If vol is high, is it a good time for the client to buy?
questions.a:
**Yes — high vol is favorable for the client.** High vol inflates the call premium → bigger discount → cheaper entry → higher max return. The client monetizes high vol by selling it (short call).

**For the trader**: high vol means the long call in the hedge is expensive to buy. But the trader also received a higher certificate price from the client. The net depends on the trader's vol forecast vs implied vol — if the trader thinks realized vol will be lower than implied, the trade is profitable.

turbo.title: Trading Q&A — Turbo Certificates
turbo.subtitle: Turbo Long = Long Stock − PV(Strike). Knock-out at barrier. Delta ≈ 1 (leveraged exposure).

questions.q: I sold 1 000 Turbo Long (ratio 0.1). How many stocks do I need to buy to be hedged?
questions.a:
**Buy 100 stocks.**

A Turbo Long has delta ≈ 1 per unit, but the ratio converts units to shares.

1 000 Turbos × ratio 0.1 = **100 shares** of delta exposure. You sold → you are short delta → buy 100 stocks to hedge.

questions.q: For a given underlying, I have 2 Turbos: one with leverage 5, the other with leverage 10. Which Turbo is cheaper?
questions.a:
**Leverage 10 is cheaper.**

Higher leverage = barrier is closer to spot = higher probability of knock-out. The client is taking more risk → pays less.

Turbo price ≈ (Spot − Strike) × Ratio. Higher leverage means the strike is closer to spot → smaller (S − K) → **cheaper Turbo**.

questions.q: For a given underlying, I have 2 Turbos: one with 1% barrier distance, the other with 10% barrier distance. Which is more expensive?
questions.a:
**The 10% barrier distance is more expensive.**

10% barrier distance means the barrier is further from spot → the Turbo is more in-the-money → lower probability of knock-out → more protection → **higher price**.

1% barrier distance = barrier very close to spot = high KO probability = cheap.

questions.q: For a Turbo Long, do we increase or decrease the strike and barrier every day? Why?
questions.a:
**Increase them every day.**

The issuer hedges by holding stocks. To finance this stock position, the issuer borrows cash. The cost of this financing must be passed on to the client.

The strike increases daily by the financing cost: ΔK = K × r × (1/365).

The barrier moves up with the strike. This is the **daily funding adjustment** — the client pays for the carry of the hedge through a slowly rising strike.

questions.q: If a Turbo Long gets knocked out, what is the residual value?
questions.a:
**Residual value = (S − K) × Ratio × FX**

When the barrier is breached, the Turbo is terminated. But the barrier is set above the strike (B > K for a Turbo Long), so there is still intrinsic value left:

- S = spot at knock-out (≈ barrier level)
- K = strike (below the barrier)
- Ratio = conversion ratio
- FX = currency adjustment (if underlying is in a different currency)

The residual value is the remaining intrinsic value returned to the holder. It can be zero if the stock gaps through both barrier and strike.

questions.q: A Turbo Long (10 000 lots) gets knocked out. What must the trader do?
questions.a:
**Sell all the stocks he was holding against this Turbo.**

The trader (issuer) was hedged by holding the underlying shares. When the Turbo gets KO'd, the product is dead — there is no more delta exposure to hedge. The trader must **unwind the hedge** by selling all the stocks.

10 000 lots × ratio = number of shares to sell. This must be done quickly, but the stock is already falling (that's why it hit the barrier) — selling into a falling market adds further pressure.

bonus_cert.title: Trading Q&A — Bonus Certificates
bonus_cert.subtitle: Replication: BC = Long S + Long Put D&O(K=Bonus, B=Barrier) − Call(K=Cap). **Client** buys the cert (Long S, Long Put D&O, Short Call). **Trader** sells the cert and hedges (Short S, Short Put D&O, Long Call).

questions.q: Which is more expensive: a Bonus Certificate or a Capped Bonus Certificate?
questions.a:
**The uncapped Bonus Certificate is more expensive.**

- **Client view:** the capped version includes a short call (K=Cap) — the client sells upside. That call premium reduces the certificate price. Lower cap → cheaper cert → more limited upside.
- **Trader view:** in the capped version, the trader is **long the call**. This call premium is income that offsets the cost of the put D&O the trader is short. Without the cap, the trader has no call income and must charge a higher price to compensate for the full put D&O exposure.

The cap finances the bonus protection. No cap = the client pays for the full put D&O.

questions.q: As a trader (seller), where are you gamma long / gamma short?
questions.a:
The trader sold the cert → the trader's hedge is: **Short S, Short Put D&O, Long Call(K=Cap).**

- **Near the cap:** the trader is **gamma long** (long call). Spot moves around the cap generate mark-to-market gains. The trader pays theta for this.
- **Far from barrier, near the bonus level:** the trader is **short the Put D&O** → **gamma short**. The put behaves like a vanilla put here → negative gamma for the trader.
- **Near the barrier:** the Put D&O's gamma flips violently. The trader (short the put) faces **massive positive gamma** as the put collapses — but this is unstable and discontinuous. The delta of the put swings wildly near B → hedging is extremely difficult.

**The barrier zone is the most dangerous for the trader.** Gamma changes sign, delta is unstable, and a barrier breach causes a discrete jump in the position value.

questions.q: Client vs Trader: who is long vega, who is short vega?
questions.a:
**It depends on where spot is. And client and trader are always opposite.**

**Client (long the cert):**
- Far from barrier: **long vega** (the Put D&O gains value with higher vol)
- Near the barrier: **short vega** (higher vol → more likely to hit barrier → put loses value)
- Near the cap: **short vega** (short call)

**Trader (short the cert):**
- Far from barrier: **short vega** (short Put D&O loses with higher vol)
- Near the barrier: **long vega** (the put the trader is short loses value → trader benefits)
- Near the cap: **long vega** (long call)

This **vega sign flip near the barrier** is the hallmark of barrier options. The trader can go from short vega to long vega just because the stock dropped 5%.

questions.q: If volatility increases, does the certificate get cheaper or more expensive?
questions.a:
**It depends on barrier distance — and the answer is different for client and trader.**

- **Barrier far (30% OTM):** Put D&O ≈ vanilla put → higher vol increases the put → cert gets **more expensive** (client's long put gains, trader's short put loses).
- **Barrier close (5% OTM):** higher vol → much more likely to knock out the put → put *loses* value → cert gets **cheaper** (client loses, trader benefits).

The vol sensitivity chart often shows a **non-monotonic** curve: price rises with vol initially, then reverses as the barrier knock-out probability dominates.

**For the trader**: being short vol far from barrier but long vol near barrier creates a natural but unstable hedge — the vol exposure flips as the stock moves.

questions.q: What happens to the trader's hedge as spot approaches the barrier?
questions.a:
**The trader faces a hedging nightmare.** The trader is short the Put D&O. As S → B:

- The Put D&O collapses toward zero → the trader benefits (short position gains).
- **But:** delta swings violently. The put's delta flips from negative to near-zero → the trader must rapidly unwind their stock hedge (buy back shares).
- **Gamma is extreme and unstable.** A small move can cause huge P&L swings.
- **At the barrier breach:** the put dies instantly → discrete jump in position value. The trader goes from "short a put + hedged" to "flat" in one tick.

This is why barrier options are exotic — not because of the payoff, but because the **Greeks near the barrier are brutal to manage**. The trader needs to pre-position for the barrier event, often at a cost.

questions.q: When does the Bonus Cert outperform the stock? (Client perspective)
questions.a:
**In sideways or moderately bearish markets** (barrier intact):

| Scenario | Stock P&L | Cert P&L | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| S_T < Barrier (breach) | S_T − S₀ (loss) | S_T − Cost (same loss) | Tie |
| Barrier < S_T < Bonus | S_T − S₀ (loss) | Bonus − Cost (protected) | **Cert** |
| Bonus < S_T < Cap | S_T − S₀ | S_T − Cost | Similar |
| S_T > Cap | S_T − S₀ (big gain) | Cap − Cost (capped) | **Stock** |

The sweet spot for the client: stock drops moderately but stays above the barrier. The worst case: stock crashes through the barrier (bonus vanishes, full downside exposure).

questions.q: Continuous vs discrete barrier: who benefits?
questions.a:
**Continuous monitoring makes the cert cheaper** → benefits the **trader/issuer**.

- Continuous: more chances to knock out during the life → Put D&O is worth less → the cert price is lower → issuer margin is easier to extract.
- Discrete (closing prices only): the put survives intraday breaches → it's worth more → cert is more expensive to structure.

**For the trader:** continuous barrier is easier to price (Reiner-Rubinstein closed form) but harder to hedge (barrier can be hit at any time). Discrete barrier requires Monte Carlo or lattice methods (Broadie-Glasserman-Kou correction) but hedging is more predictable (only check at close).

Most Bonus Certificates use **continuous** monitoring.

questions.q: Compare delta-hedging a Long Put Down-and-In (PDI) vs a Short Put Down-and-Out (PDO) as spot falls from 100 to 70 (barrier). What is similar?
questions.a:
**Both positions accumulate shares on the way down and must sell at the barrier — creating identical pin risk.**

| Spot | Long PDI | Short PDO |
|---|---|---|
| **100 (start)** | Buy ~5 shares (Δ ≈ −0.05, knock-in unlikely) | Sell ~45 shares (Δ ≈ +0.45, behaves like short ATM put) |
| **85 (−15%)** | Buy ~15 more (Δ deepens to −0.20) | Buy back ~15 (Δ shrinks to +0.30, knock-out more likely) |
| **75 (−25%)** | Buy ~40 more (Δ ≈ −0.55, barrier proximity amplifies) | Buy back ~25 more (Δ ≈ +0.05, nearly zero) |
| **70 (breach)** | PDI activates → vanilla put ITM → sell ~100+ excess shares | PDO knocks out → obligation dead → sell entire accumulated hedge |

**Key similarity:** both sell at the same level, at the same moment, potentially in a thin market. This is the **barrier pin risk**.

questions.q: A trader is short a Put Down-and-Out. Is the trader gamma short throughout the life of the trade?
questions.a:
**No — this is a critical misconception.** Being short a barrier option does **not** mean you are gamma short throughout.

The Short PDO starts **gamma short** (behaving like a short vanilla put far from the barrier). But as spot approaches the barrier, gamma **flips to long**.

**Why?** Near the barrier, every tick down increases the probability of knock-out and liberation. The put is losing value rapidly as it approaches death → the trader (short the put) benefits from this convexity. The position is effectively long gamma near the barrier.

**Gamma sign on a barrier option is dynamic and path-dependent**, not fixed by the initial direction of the trade. This is what makes barrier options exotic — the Greeks change nature depending on where spot is relative to the barrier.

questions.q: Spot breaches the barrier at 70. Describe precisely what happens to the hedge for: (a) Long PDI, (b) Short PDO.
questions.a:
**(a) Long PDI — PDI activates → vanilla put ITM:**
- The barrier delta (−2 / −3 near the barrier) snaps back to vanilla put delta (≈ −0.88).
- The trader held ~200 shares, now only needs ~88 → must **sell ~100+ shares** immediately.
- Delta is **compressed brutally**. Gamma shows a **violent discontinuity**.
- After breach: the trader manages a live vanilla put ITM. Work continues — spot falls further → buy, spot rebounds → sell. **Gamma short (vanilla), vega short (vanilla).**

**(b) Short PDO — PDO knocks out → obligation extinguished:**
- The put ceases to exist → delta collapses to 0 instantly.
- Every share bought back during the descent must now be sold. **Full unwind** — not just the last few shares, the **entire hedge position**.
- After breach: **nothing to do**. Position is dead — premium kept, fully released.
- **Delta = 0, Gamma = 0, Vega = 0.**

The PDI trader still has work after breach; the PDO trader is done but had to dump all shares at once.

questions.q: Near the barrier, how do gamma and vega behave differently for a Long PDI vs a Short PDO?
questions.a:
**Both flip — but in opposite ways.**

| Greek | Long PDI (at 75, barrier 70) | Short PDO (at 75, barrier 70) |
|---|---|---|
| **Gamma** | **Strong long** — barrier proximity amplifies convexity, every tick increases knock-in probability | **Flips to long!** — the put is dying, short position benefits from the rapid value collapse |
| **Vega** | **Strong long** — higher vol increases knock-in probability → put gains value | **Flips to long!** — higher vol increases knock-out probability → the put the trader is short loses value → trader benefits |

**After breach:**
- Long PDI → gamma short (vanilla put), vega short (vanilla put)
- Short PDO → gamma = 0, vega = 0 (position dead)

The flip in gamma and vega near the barrier is the hallmark of barrier options. It's why standard intuition ("short option = short gamma") breaks down near barriers.

questions.q: Why is barrier pin risk so dangerous for both Long PDI and Short PDO traders?
questions.a:
**Both traders are forced sellers of stock at the exact same price, at the exact same moment, in a potentially illiquid market.**

- **Long PDI trader:** held ~200 shares (delta hedge for near-barrier PDI), needs only ~88 after activation → must sell ~100+ shares.
- **Short PDO trader:** accumulated ~45 shares bought back on the way down, needs 0 after knock-out → must sell all ~45 shares.

**The danger:**
1. **Concentration:** every dealer hedging similar barriers at 70 hits the same trigger simultaneously.
2. **Illiquidity:** the stock is already falling (that's why it reached the barrier). Adding sell pressure into a falling market amplifies the move.
3. **Feedback loop:** barrier breach → forced selling → price drops further → triggers more barriers on other products → more forced selling.

This is why barriers at round numbers (70, 80, 90) create **market structure risk** — it's not one trader's problem, it's a systemic hedging flow.

questions.q: How do you make a currency swap? Give an example.
questions.a:
You simultaneously **buy spot and sell forward** (or sell spot and buy forward).

**Example:** I am in Europe. I buy a USD bond (6M maturity, $1M notional). I need dollars now.

1. **Today:** I sell EUR/USD spot (= sell EUR, buy USD) → I get the dollars to buy the bond.
2. **In 6 months:** I will receive dollars back (bond matures). But the risk is those dollars lose value against EUR.
3. **Today (hedge):** I sell USD forward 6M = buy EUR/USD forward (= buy EUR, sell USD).

An FX swap is nothing more than a **simultaneous buy spot + sell forward** transaction.

questions.q: How do you make an interest rate swap?
questions.a:
When you **buy** a bond, you receive the coupon. When you **sell/issue** a bond, you pay the coupon.

Suppose you pay 6% fixed. The risk is that rates decrease — you could be paying less. So you would prefer to **receive 6% fixed** and **pay a lower floating rate**.

**Execution:** Buy a bond with 6% fixed coupon, and sell (issue) a Floating Rate Note (FRN).

The PV of both legs must be equal (price matching ensures cash flow equivalence). The swap transforms your fixed exposure into floating.

questions.q: If I hedge my coupon with a swap, am I still exposed to the price risk of the bond if the curve moves?
questions.a:
**No.** By swapping fixed coupons against floating, you **neutralize your duration**. The P&L from the swap's mark-to-market compensates the variation of the bond price.

You are switching from a **price exposure** to a **margin exposure**:

**Fixed Bond + Pay-Fixed Swap = Synthetic FRN**

By hedging the coupon, you are "killing" the duration. Without duration, there is no yield risk. Your bond's price becomes stable, just like a bank deposit.

questions.q: I am swap payer (I pay fixed) on 100M notional. Rates increase by 10 bps. Do I make or lose money?
questions.a:
**You make money.** As a payer, you are short the fixed bond (you benefit when fixed bond prices fall, i.e., when rates rise).

Approximate P&L = Notional × DV01 × Δbps = ~**100K** (depending on the duration of the swap).

questions.q: If I pay the swap (pay fixed), do I increase or decrease my duration?
questions.a:
**You decrease it.** Paying fixed = selling a fixed bond + buying an FRN. The FRN resets at every coupon period (typically quarterly), so its duration is near zero.

Net effect: you removed the fixed-rate duration from your portfolio.

questions.q: What is the duration of a 5Y zero-coupon bond? Why?
questions.a:
**5 years.** A zero-coupon bond has no intermediate cash flows — all the risk is concentrated at maturity. Duration = maturity for a ZCB.

This is also why ZCBs have the highest duration (and convexity) for a given maturity compared to coupon bonds.

questions.q: What does it mean buying a bond at −1% interest rate? Why would you do it?
questions.a:
It means you **buy at ~101 something that will return 100** at maturity. You are guaranteed to lose money if held to maturity.

**Why buy it?** If you think rates will go **even more negative**. The bond price will rise further, and you can sell it at a profit before maturity. This happened extensively in Europe and Japan (2014–2022).

questions.q: Can a zero-coupon bond trade at a premium? Why?
questions.a:
**Yes.** If demand pushes yields into negative territory, the ZCB price rises above par.

Price = Face / (1 + y)^T. If y < 0, then (1 + y)^T < 1, so Price > Face.

This happened with German Bunds and Swiss government bonds during the negative rate era.

questions.q: Describe simply what convexity is in bond trading.
questions.a:
**With convexity, you earn more when you make money, and you lose less when you lose money.**

Convexity is the curvature of the price-yield relationship. A bond with high convexity benefits more from a rate decrease than it suffers from an equal rate increase. It's the bond equivalent of being "long gamma" in options.

questions.q: What is the cheapest: a callable bond or a straight bond? Why?
questions.a:
**The callable bond is cheaper.**

A callable bond has **negative convexity at low yields**: the issuer calls it back to refinance at lower rates. By doing so, the issuer prevents the bondholder from participating in the full price appreciation.

Callable Bond = Straight Bond − Call Option (issuer holds the call).

The bondholder is short convexity at low yields → they must be compensated with a lower price (higher yield).

questions.q: For a given maturity, which bond has the strongest convexity: a ZCB or a 5% coupon bond?
questions.a:
**The ZCB.**

Visualize the PV of a bond as a histogram: X-axis = year, Y-axis = % of total PV each cash flow represents. Draw a curve connecting the tops.

- **Coupon bond:** PV is spread across many dates → the curve is flat.
- **ZCB:** 100% of PV is at maturity → the curve is a single spike at the end.

The more the value is "pushed" into the future, the steeper the sensitivity to rate changes → **higher convexity**.

questions.q: If interest rates increase, does convexity increase or decrease? Why?
questions.a:
**It decreases.** When rates rise, the present value of each cash flow decreases — especially the longest-dated ones (because they are discounted more heavily).

Since convexity is driven by distant cash flows, and those lose the most PV when rates rise, the overall convexity shrinks.

questions.q: Two bonds, same maturity: one with 5% coupon, the other with 10% coupon. Rates go from 2% to 8%. Which one sees its convexity decrease first?
questions.a:
**The 5% coupon bond**, because it has **higher duration**.

Higher duration means greater sensitivity to rate changes. When rates rise sharply, the 5% bond's longer-duration cash flows lose more PV, causing its convexity to drop faster than the 10% bond (which has more PV in near-term coupons).

questions.q: Two portfolios: (A) 2 bonds with 10Y duration each, (B) 1 bond with 5Y duration + 1 bond with 15Y duration. Which has the biggest convexity?
questions.a:
**Portfolio B** has more convexity.

Both portfolios have the same average duration (10Y). But portfolio B has cash flows that are **more spread out** over time (5Y and 15Y vs two 10Y).

Convexity is a function of the **dispersion** of cash flows around the duration. More spread = more convexity. This is the principle behind **barbell vs bullet** strategies: the barbell (B) always has higher convexity for the same duration.

interview_greeks.title: Greeks — Reading Your Book
interview_greeks.subtitle: Quick-fire questions on Delta, Gamma, Vega, and P&L from a trading book perspective.

questions.q: I see in my book: Delta = 0, Gamma = 1 M EUR. Spot increases by 5%. What is my Delta?
questions.a:
**Delta = 5 M EUR.**

Gamma tells you how much your delta changes per 1% move in spot.

- Gamma = 1 M EUR → for every 1% up, delta increases by 1 M.
- Spot moves up 5% → ΔDelta = Gamma × move = 1 M × 5 = **5 M EUR**.
- Starting delta was 0 → new delta = 0 + 5 M = **5 M EUR**.

You are now long 5 M EUR of delta — the market moved in your favor (long gamma = you accumulate delta in the direction of the move).

questions.q: I see in my book: Delta = −1 M EUR, Gamma = 1 M EUR. Spot goes up by 1%. What is my Delta?
questions.a:
**Delta = −2 M EUR.**

New Delta = Old Delta − Gamma × %move = −1 M − 1 M × 1 = **−2 M EUR**.

Gamma = 1 M means your delta changes by 1 M for each 1% move. Starting at −1 M delta, a 1% up move pushes delta further negative by another 1 M.

questions.q: I see in my book: Delta = 1 M EUR, Gamma = −1 M EUR. What is my Delta if spot goes down 1%? Up 1%?
questions.a:
**Down 1% → Delta = 2 M EUR. Up 1% → Delta = 0.**

You are **short gamma** (Gamma = −1 M). Short gamma means your delta moves *against* you:

- **Spot down 1%:** New Delta = 1 M − (−1 M) × (−1%) = 1 M + 1 M = **2 M EUR**. You are getting longer as the market falls — buying into a falling market.
- **Spot up 1%:** New Delta = 1 M − (−1 M) × (+1%) = 1 M − 1 M = **0 EUR**. You are getting flatter as the market rises — losing your long as it rallies.

**This is the curse of short gamma:** you always end up on the wrong side. You accumulate delta in the losing direction.

questions.q: I see 100 K EUR vega in my book. IV drops by 1 point. Do I make or lose money? How much?
questions.a:
**You lose 100 K EUR.**

Vega = sensitivity of your position to a 1-point (1 vol point) change in implied volatility.

- Vega = +100 K → you are **long vol**. You benefit when IV rises, you lose when IV falls.
- IV drops 1 point → P&L = Vega × ΔIV = 100 K × (−1) = **−100 K EUR**.

Long vega = long options (you paid premium, you want vol to expand). A drop in IV compresses your option values → loss.

questions.q: I have Gamma = 1 M, Delta flat. Spot goes up by 5%. What is my P&L?
questions.a:
**P&L = +125 K EUR.**

The gamma P&L formula is:

**P&L = ½ × Gamma × (move)²**

- Gamma = 1 M EUR per 1% move
- Move = 5%
- P&L = ½ × 1 M × 5% × 5 = ½ × 5 M × 5% = **125 K EUR**

Or equivalently: ½ × 1 M × (5)² × 1% = ½ × 1 M × 25% = **125 K EUR**.

You started delta flat (delta = 0). As spot rose, gamma built up your delta in the right direction. The P&L is the area under the delta curve — hence the ½ × Γ × (Δs)² formula. **Long gamma + big move = profit**, regardless of direction.

questions.q: I buy 1 000 calls, ATM, Multiplier 10. How many shares do I buy/sell to be hedged?
questions.a:
**Sell 5 000 shares.**

ATM call → Delta ≈ 0.5. You are long 1 000 calls × multiplier 10 = exposure on 10 000 shares.

Delta exposure = 10 000 × 0.5 = **5 000 shares long**.

To be delta-neutral, sell 5 000 shares against your calls.

questions.q: I have a deep ITM call. The dividend is about to be paid out. What should I do?
questions.a:
**Exercise your calls to own the stock before ex-date.**

If you hold a deep ITM call and don't exercise, the stock drops by the dividend amount on the ex-date — and you receive nothing. Your call loses value by roughly the dividend amount.

By exercising early, you own the stock and **collect the dividend**. The time value of a deep ITM call is minimal anyway, so you sacrifice almost nothing by exercising. Not exercising = negative P&L impact for missing the dividend.

questions.q: Expiry day. I am short 1 000 calls, strike 100, on a stock with spot = 100. What does my book show as delta cash if spot is at 99.9? At 100.1?
questions.a:
**Spot = 99.9 → Delta cash = 0. Spot = 100.1 → Delta cash = −10 000 000.**

- At 99.9: calls expire OTM → delta = 0. No exposure.
- At 100.1: calls expire ITM → delta = −1 (you are short calls). You are short 1 000 × multiplier × spot = **−10 M EUR** of delta.

This is **pin risk** at expiry. A 0.2 move around the strike swings your delta from 0 to −10 M. At expiry, gamma on ATM options goes to infinity — this is the most dangerous moment for short option positions.

questions.q: I buy a stock at 100. It goes up 10% day 1, then down 10% day 2. Did I make money, lose money, or breakeven?
questions.a:
**You lost 1 EUR. Ending spot = 99.**

- Day 1: 100 × 1.10 = **110**
- Day 2: 110 × 0.90 = **99**

Percentage moves are not symmetric. A +10% followed by −10% does not bring you back to the start. The loss is always 100 × (move)² = 100 × (10%)² = **1 EUR**. This is the **volatility drag** — the reason why leveraged and volatile assets underperform over time, even if the average return is zero.

questions.q: I have an ITM call. IV goes to 0. What is my Delta?
questions.a:
**Delta = 1.**

If volatility is zero, there is no uncertainty. An ITM call will finish ITM with certainty → it behaves like a forward. Delta = 1.

questions.q: I have an OTM call. IV goes to 0. What is my Delta?
questions.a:
**Delta = 0.**

If volatility is zero, an OTM call has zero probability of ever reaching the strike. It will expire worthless with certainty. No sensitivity to spot → Delta = 0.

questions.q: I am Long K1 calls, Short K2 calls (K1 < K2). What is my skew position?
questions.a:
**You are long skew.**

You want IV at K1 to increase and IV at K2 to decrease — i.e., you want the skew to **steepen**.

- Long K1 = long vega at K1 → you benefit if IV at K1 rises.
- Short K2 = short vega at K2 → you benefit if IV at K2 falls.

A steeper skew (lower strikes trading at higher IV relative to higher strikes) helps both legs. You are **long skew**.

questions.q: Why am I rho positive when I buy a call?
questions.a:
**Two ways to see it:**

**1. Forward argument:** A call is long the forward. F = S × e^{(r−q)T}. If you increase the rate, the forward goes up → call value increases.

**2. Cash argument:** When you buy a call, you delta-hedge by selling stock. Selling stock generates cash that you deposit in a bank. You want the highest possible rate on that cash → **you are rho positive**.

Both views give the same result: long call = benefits from higher rates.

questions.q: If volatility goes to infinity, what happens to Gamma?
questions.a:
**Gamma goes to 0.**

If volatility is infinite, every option has the same probability of finishing ITM: 50%. All deltas converge to 0.5, regardless of strike.

If all deltas are 0.5 at every strike level, they don't change when spot moves. Gamma is the change of delta → **gamma = 0**.

questions.q: If volatility is 0, what is Gamma for an ATM option? And for OTM/ITM?
questions.a:
**ATM → Gamma goes to infinity. OTM/ITM → Gamma = 0.**

With zero vol, delta is a step function: 0 below the strike, 1 above. At the strike (ATM), delta jumps discontinuously from 0 to 1 over an infinitely small range → **gamma = infinity**.

For OTM: delta is stuck at 0, never changes → gamma = 0.
For ITM: delta is stuck at 1, never changes → gamma = 0.

This is the mirror image of the infinite vol case. At vol = 0, all the gamma concentrates at the strike. At vol = ∞, gamma vanishes everywhere.

interview_it.title: IT & Project Setup — Why It Matters on a Trading Desk
interview_it.subtitle: Practical questions about Python tooling, version control, and project architecture — with trading desk examples.

questions.q: What is a virtual environment (venv)? Why do we use one?
questions.a:
A venv is an isolated Python installation specific to your project.

**Example:** You built a risk report last year using `pandas 2.1`. Today you start a new pricer that needs `pandas 2.2`. You run `pip install --upgrade pandas` — and your risk report breaks because `pandas 2.2` removed a function you were using. Now the desk has no risk report on a Monday morning.

With a venv, each project has its own isolated copy of pandas. Upgrading one project doesn't touch the other. The risk report keeps running on 2.1, the pricer runs on 2.2. No one gets fired.

questions.q: What is requirements.txt? Why is it important?
questions.a:
It lists every package your project needs, with versions. When someone clones the project, `pip install -r requirements.txt` installs everything in one command.

**Example:** A new intern joins the desk. Without `requirements.txt`, they clone the repo, run `streamlit run app.py`, and get `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'plotly'`. They install plotly. Then scipy is missing. Then numpy. Then the wrong version of streamlit breaks the layout. They waste half a day.

With `requirements.txt`, it's one command: done. They're productive in 5 minutes.

questions.q: What is .gitignore? Why do we need it?
questions.a:
It tells Git which files to never track. You list patterns (`venv/`, `*.pyc`, `.env`) and Git ignores them completely.

**Example:** Your `.env` file contains the API key for your Bloomberg data feed. Without `.gitignore`, you push it to GitHub. A bot scrapes it within minutes. Someone runs 50,000 queries on your key overnight. You get a $30,000 invoice and the API key is revoked. The desk has no market data the next morning.

`.gitignore` prevents this. It keeps secrets, generated files, and local junk out of the repository.

questions.q: Why do we never push the venv/ folder to Git?
questions.a:
Three reasons:

1. **Size** — your code is 200 KB. The venv is 300 MB. That's like emailing a 10-page PDF with 500 MB of attachments.
2. **OS-specific** — a venv built on Windows won't work on the Linux server where your app runs in production. It contains compiled binaries tied to your machine.
3. **Redundant** — `requirements.txt` already describes everything. Anyone can recreate the venv in 10 seconds.

**Example:** You wouldn't ship your entire Bloomberg terminal to a colleague so they can read one Excel file. You send the file and let them open it on their own terminal.

questions.q: Why do we separate engines (computation) from UI (Streamlit)?
questions.a:
So that different people can work on different parts. A quant can improve the Reiner-Rubinstein formula in `engines/bonus.py` without knowing Streamlit. A developer can redesign the charts without touching the math.

**Example:** On a desk, the structurer designs the product payoff, the quant builds the pricing model, and the IT dev builds the booking system. The structurer doesn't write C++, the quant doesn't do HTML. If the quant changes the pricing model, the booking system shouldn't break. Same principle: `engines/` is the quant, `ui/` is the IT dev.

questions.q: What is Git? Why not just share code on a shared drive or by email?
questions.a:
Git tracks every change, who made it, when, and why. You can revert to any previous version.

**Example:** Two quants work on the same pricer. Quant A modifies the vol surface. Quant B modifies the dividend model. On a shared drive, whoever saves last overwrites the other's work. With email, nobody knows which version is current — "pricer_v3_final_FINAL_v2.py".

With Git, both quants work on their own branch, and Git merges the changes together. If Quant A's vol change breaks something, you revert to the previous version in one command. Try doing that with a shared drive.

questions.q: What is a commit? What is a push?
questions.a:
A **commit** = saving a snapshot with a message ("fixed barrier pricing bug"). A **push** = uploading that snapshot to GitHub so others can see it.

**Example:** You fix a bug in the pricer at 6pm. You commit but forget to push. Your laptop gets stolen on the way home. The fix is gone. The desk trades with the buggy pricer the next morning.

Commit = writing in your notebook. Push = publishing it. Always push before you leave.

questions.q: Why do we use @st.cache_data instead of recomputing everything each time?
questions.a:
Streamlit re-runs the entire script on every user interaction. Without caching, moving a volatility slider recomputes every chart from scratch — the app freezes for 3 seconds each time.

**Example:** A sales desk asks you for the price of a Bonus Certificate. You compute it in 2 seconds. They ask "same thing but bump the vol by 1%?". Without caching, you recompute everything from zero — underlying PV, barrier pricing, payoff charts, all of it. With caching, only the vol-dependent parts recompute. The rest is instant.

It's like a trader who keeps their pricing sheet open instead of rebuilding it from scratch for every client call.

questions.q: Why is the project structured in folders (engines/, ui/, data/, tests/) instead of one big file?
questions.a:
One 1,900-line file works but is painful to navigate, and every Git merge creates conflicts.

**Example:** Imagine the entire desk — traders, structurers, risk, compliance — shares one single Excel workbook. Every time someone updates a cell, it conflicts with someone else's change. Finding anything takes 5 minutes of scrolling. Now imagine splitting it into tabs by function: Pricing, Risk, P&L, Compliance. Each team owns their tab. That's what folders do for code.

questions.q: What are tests? Why do we have tests/test_engines.py?
questions.a:
Tests are automated checks that verify your code still works after a change. Our tests check: "does put-call parity hold?", "is American price ≥ European?", "does a par bond price at 1000?".

**Example:** A junior quant "optimizes" the Black-Scholes function and accidentally breaks the delta calculation. Without tests, nobody notices until a trader hedges with the wrong delta and loses €200K. With tests, the junior runs `pytest` before pushing — it fails in 2 seconds, they fix the bug, the desk never sees it.

Tests are your pre-trade checks. You run them before deploying, not after the P&L blows up.
